All right, now we are going to get into the logistics of weigh-in day and how we are going to run it. So, number one, the duration should be 30 to 60 minutes and this depends on how comfortable you are with the process and how much time you want to spend in your, primarily in your supplement sale and how you're presenting it. But it's important that you get the clothes and sell the supplements. So, if you're more experienced, maybe you can knock them out in 30 minutes. If you are less experienced, 60 minutes, giving yourself a little bit more time to complete the process until you get more experienced. You could always break it in the middle and go 45 minutes as well. Scheduling, when do you want to do weigh-in day? You basically want to do it as soon as you can after the sale because this is a one-on-one appointment, which we'll get to. But you do not have to set aside a large amount of time and make sure your gym is empty and in all of that stuff. This is just the first appointment of what are going to be bi-weekly appointments with this particular client. So this just happens to be their first one and you want to block it out in your schedule because eventually you'll just be having meetings, you know, back to back to back to back to back. Some of them will be bi-weekly meetings, some of them will be weigh-in, some will be halfway meetings, some will be weigh-outs, but you won't have to worry about all of the training if you are running full hybrid and blocking big chunks of time apart because what you're primarily doing would be meeting with people either 30, 60, or 15 minutes if it's a normal bi-weekly meeting. So you do want to get them in as soon as possible. Just make sure your calendar is available and schedule them after the sale to get them started. How many per time slot? One. You want to do it one-on-one because you are selling a one-on-one program. You are selling a private program that you are going to be, and they might be in a Facebook group with other people doing the same program, but this is not a large group program. This is a one-on-one experience for them. So you want to kick it off the right way by giving them a one-on-one experience. Who should do this? And how much should you pay them? We recommend paying them hourly. You do need to pay people if they are working, so you can't just not pay them and have them only work on commission or something like that because if they don't get any sales in this particular role or at least check with your state laws. But who's doing it? It should be the person that is going to coach them throughout their entire process. So if that is the owner, then it's the owner. If it is a trainer or a coach, it is the coach. But I don't recommend like an administrative person like an ops manager or something doing this process because it doesn't leave the best impression if somebody else besides their coach is getting them started and setting their plan and all of that stuff. This is supposed to be a very personalized program. It's not a large group. So the coach or whoever is going to be facilitating the coaching should be the one doing the way in as well because they're really explaining everything, setting the game plan, talking to them about what they're going to follow up with two weeks later, doing all of that process and that should be the person that's recommending their supplement regimen and is going to downsell them into different supplements or keep that going at least. So that's the person that should be getting the commission and continually working with the person. So if they do sell supplements, we recommend that you pay out a 7.5% commission. You can pay whatever you want, but that is just our recommendation. And that turns out to be in really great money. So you should really have your coaches want to do this. They should be super pumped up to do this because it's far more than they can make just training in general. So as an example, if you sell three people at an average of 200 an hour, just as an example, their take on that, which three people at an average of 200 per hour is not hard at all. That's one four pack per hour, right? Their take on it, if they sell three of them at that four pack would be $45 per hour, which is typically probably higher than what most gym owners are paying their coaches. So as long as they stay consistent and they're good at sales or at least moderate at sales, this should be the most profitable day for them. So that is why they would love to coach as many clients as they possibly can. So that's kind of the logistics of Wayenday. It's different than large group wayendays for sure. But we will get into the specifics of how it's different and what comes up after this part in the next videos.
